guide/festivals.json.title: Festivals & events
guide/festivals.json.subtitle: 42 celebrations through the year — pick a day to see what's on.

festivals.name: New Year's Day
festivals.description: National public holiday marking the start of the new year, celebrated across Switzerland with family gatherings and festive traditions.

festivals.name: Vogel Gryff
festivals.description: A unique Basel carnival featuring three processions with the mythical figures of the Griffin, the Lion, and the Wild Man, representing Basel's guilds and traditions.

festivals.name: Épiphanie Festival
festivals.description: A religious celebration in the Jura region marking the revelation of Jesus to the Magi, featuring traditional processions and local customs.

festivals.name: Unspunnen Festival
festivals.description: Held every 12 years, this iconic festival celebrates Swiss Alpine traditions with yodelling, wrestling, and traditional costume parades in the Bernese Oberland.

festivals.name: Labour Day
festivals.description: National public holiday honouring workers and the labour movement, observed across Switzerland with parades and celebrations.

festivals.name: Ascension Day
festivals.description: A national public holiday in Switzerland's Christian tradition, celebrating Christ's ascension to heaven 39 days after Easter.

festivals.name: Whit Monday
festivals.description: National public holiday commemorating the Holy Spirit's descent upon the apostles, observed 50 days after Easter.

festivals.name: Montreux Jazz Festival
festivals.description: One of the world's most famous jazz festivals, held on the shores of Lake Geneva with performances from international jazz legends and emerging artists.

festivals.name: Verbier Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious international classical music festival in the Alpine resort of Verbier, featuring world-class orchestras and soloists.

festivals.name: Paléo Festival
festivals.description: Switzerland's largest open-air music festival held on the shores of Lake Geneva, featuring rock, pop, indie and world music acts.

festivals.name: Gstaad Menuhin Festival
festivals.description: One of Europe's leading classical music festivals, held in the elegant Alpine resort of Gstaad with chamber music and orchestral performances.

festivals.name: Swiss National Day
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating Swiss independence and the founding of the Swiss Confederation with bonfires, parades, and fireworks across all regions.

festivals.name: Lucerne Festival
festivals.description: A world-renowned classical music festival held in Lucerne's stunning concert halls and lakeside venues, featuring leading orchestras and soloists.

festivals.name: OpenAir St. Gallen
festivals.description: A major open-air music and culture festival in eastern Switzerland featuring rock, pop, indie and theatre performances in a beautiful natural setting.

festivals.name: Zurich Street Parade
festivals.description: Europe's largest electronic dance music festival and parade, attracting hundreds of thousands to Zurich's streets for performances and celebrations.

festivals.name: Grape Harvest Festival
festivals.description: A vibrant celebration of the wine harvest season in Neuchâtel with parades, wine tastings, and traditional grape-pressing ceremonies.

festivals.name: Fête des Vendanges
festivals.description: The Lavaux wine region's celebrated harvest festival featuring traditional costumes, wine tastings, and three weeks of festivities across picturesque vineyards.

festivals.name: Zürich Film Festival
festivals.description: An international film festival showcasing new films from around the world, held in September at venues throughout Zurich.

festivals.name: Basel Fasnacht
festivals.description: Switzerland's most famous carnival, a three-day extravaganza of elaborate costumes, satirical floats, and traditional music celebrating the end of winter.

festivals.name: Lugano Festival
festivals.description: A spring music festival in the Italian-speaking region featuring classical music, contemporary works, and performances in beautiful lakeside settings.

festivals.name: Ritom Alpine Festival
festivals.description: A unique high-altitude classical music festival held at the Ritom dam in Uri, featuring intimate chamber music performances in a stunning Alpine setting.

festivals.name: Neuchâtel Lakeside Jazz Festival
festivals.description: A summer jazz festival on the shores of Lake Neuchâtel featuring international and Swiss jazz artists in a scenic lakeside venue.

festivals.name: Bellinzona Musica Festival
festivals.description: An autumn classical music festival in the Ticino region showcasing orchestras, chamber ensembles, and soloists in historic venues.

festivals.name: Schaffhausen Museum Night
festivals.description: An annual cultural event where Schaffhausen's museums stay open late with special exhibitions, performances, and guided tours throughout the night.

festivals.name: Gstaad Polo Club Tournament
festivals.description: A prestigious international polo tournament held in the exclusive Alpine resort of Gstaad, attracting world-class players and high society.

festivals.name: Christmas
festivals.description: National public holiday celebrating the birth of Christ with family traditions, carol singing, and festive gatherings across all of Switzerland.

festivals.name: Boxing Day
festivals.description: National public holiday observed across Switzerland the day after Christmas, traditionally for rest and family celebrations.

festivals.name: Appenzell New Year Festival
festivals.description: A traditional celebration of the New Year in Appenzell featuring the famous Säulirennen pig races and folk traditions unique to this Alpine region.

festivals.name: Zurich Book Fair
festivals.description: Switzerland's largest book fair and literary festival, bringing together international publishers, authors, and book lovers for readings and discussions.

festivals.name: Art Basel
festivals.description: The world's leading modern and contemporary art fair, attracting galleries and collectors from around the globe to Basel for a prestigious showcase.

festivals.name: Willisau Jazz Festival
festivals.description: An intimate jazz festival in the small town of Willisau showcasing innovative jazz musicians in a charming village setting near Lucerne.

festivals.name: St. Gallen Spring Festival
festivals.description: A cultural spring celebration featuring theatre, music, art exhibitions and street performances across St. Gallen's historic old town.

festivals.name: Geneva International Motor Show
festivals.description: One of the world's most important automobile trade shows, showcasing the latest vehicle innovations and luxury cars from global manufacturers.

festivals.name: Fribourg Medieval Festival
festivals.description: A vibrant celebration of medieval life in Fribourg's well-preserved old town with costume parades, traditional crafts, and period entertainment.

festivals.name: Locarno International Film Festival
festivals.description: A prestigious international film festival held in the sunny Ticino region, featuring world cinema, documentaries, and premieres by the shores of Lake Maggiore.

festivals.name: Aargau Wine Festival
festivals.description: A celebration of the Aargau wine region featuring tastings, vineyard tours, and culinary events showcasing local wines and traditional foods.

festivals.name: Solothurn International Film Festival
festivals.description: Switzerland's oldest and most prestigious film festival, held in January in the baroque city of Solothurn with focus on Swiss and world cinema.

festivals.name: Chur Christmas Market
festivals.description: A charming Alpine Christmas market in Switzerland's oldest city, featuring traditional crafts, mulled wine, and festive decorations in the picturesque old town.

festivals.name: Zug Carnival
festivals.description: A charming regional carnival featuring masked balls, parades, and traditional Swiss carnival traditions in the picturesque town of Zug.

festivals.name: Glarus Schenkifestival
festivals.description: A cultural festival celebrating Glarus traditions with folk music, dance, traditional food, and community celebrations in the Glarus Alps region.

festivals.name: Thurgau Wine and Harvest Festival
festivals.description: An autumn celebration of the Thurgau wine region's harvest with grape pressing, wine tastings, and regional culinary specialties.

festivals.name: Jura Linden Festival
festivals.description: A summer festival celebrating Jura's heritage with traditional music, dance, local crafts, and regional specialities in the scenic Jura mountains.

guide/history.json.title: A Short History of Switzerland
guide/history.json.subtitle: From its beginnings to today — 15 eras that shaped it.

items.label: Mesolithic Settlement
items.summary: Hunter-gatherers colonize the Swiss valleys after the ice age retreats.
items.text: As glaciers withdrew from the Alpine region, nomadic peoples followed migrating herds into what is now Switzerland. Archaeological evidence from lakeside settlements shows these early inhabitants hunted game, fished, and gathered plants across the varied landscape. The mild climate of the postglacial period made the region increasingly habitable, attracting successive waves of mobile populations who left behind stone tools and hunting equipment at sites like Schaffhausen. By 5000 BC, the population was well-established across the lowlands and lower valleys.

items.label: Neolithic Farming
items.summary: Agricultural communities build permanent settlements and develop early Alpine culture.
items.text: Farmers from the south and west introduced domesticated crops and livestock, transforming Switzerland from a hunter-gatherer economy to an agricultural one. Neolithic communities established permanent villages with wooden houses, particularly around the lakes where the famous prehistoric pile dwellings emerged. These settlements reveal sophisticated knowledge of farming, animal husbandry, and trade networks extending across Europe. Pottery, jewelry, and tools show increasing craft specialization and social organization within these early villages.

items.label: Bronze Age Expansion
items.summary: Metal-working societies flourish, establishing trade routes and regional power centers.
items.text: Bronze technology arrived with Indo-European peoples, bringing new tools, weapons, and social hierarchies to the Alpine region. The abundance of tin and copper sources made Switzerland a valuable hub in European trade networks, evidenced by rich burial sites and hoards of bronze objects. Agricultural productivity increased with improved tools, supporting larger populations and more complex societies with elite classes. By the late Bronze Age, fortified settlements emerged on hilltops, suggesting increasing competition for resources and territory.

items.label: Iron Age and Celtic Settlement
items.summary: Celtic tribes establish themselves as the dominant culture, organizing the landscape into distinct territories.
items.text: Celtic peoples migrated into Switzerland around 800 BC, bringing iron-working technology and a vibrant warrior culture that would dominate for centuries. Major Celtic tribes like the Helvetii in the central plateau, the Rhaeti in the east, and the Allobroges in the west controlled distinct regions and developed trading centers. Oppida, or fortified towns, became centers of craft, trade, and political power, connected by networks that extended across Europe. Celtic art, religious practices, and social structures deeply influenced Swiss culture, with the Helvetii becoming particularly influential and wealthy through their control of Alpine trade routes.

items.label: Roman Conquest and Integration
items.summary: Julius Caesar conquers the region and Rome transforms it into a prosperous, urbanized province.
items.text: Caesar's legions defeated the Helvetii at the Battle of Bibracte in 58 BC, bringing the Swiss territories under Roman control and ending Celtic independence. The Romans constructed towns, roads, and fortifications that connected the region to their vast empire and brought unprecedented wealth and infrastructure. Cities like Aventicum, Vindonissa, and Genava became important administrative and commercial hubs, while Roman law, language, and urban culture reshaped society. The economy flourished through wine production, agriculture, and mineral extraction, with Roman roads facilitating trade across the Alps to Italy.

items.label: Barbarian Invasions and Early Medieval Fragmentation
items.summary: Germanic tribes invade, Roman civilization collapses, and feudal kingdoms fragment the region.
items.text: As Rome weakened, Germanic peoples including the Burgundians and Alemanni migrated into Switzerland, displacing or absorbing the remaining Roman population. The infrastructure of Roman civilization crumbled, towns were abandoned, and the economy reverted to subsistence agriculture. Christianity gradually spread through the region, with monks establishing monasteries that became centers of learning and agricultural innovation. By the early medieval period, the territory was divided among competing kingdoms—Burgundy, Alemmannia, and the emerging Frankish empire—with little unified political authority.

items.label: The Holy Roman Empire and Early Cantons
items.summary: Feudal principalities rise within the Holy Roman Empire as rural communities begin organizing for mutual protection.
items.text: Switzerland remained fragmented as part of the Holy Roman Empire, ruled by various noble houses, bishops, and abbots who controlled specific regions. The Zähringen dynasty emerged as a major power, founding cities like Bern and Fribourg while extending their influence across the western plateau. Local communities in the mountain valleys—particularly Uri, Schwyz, and Unterwalden—developed autonomous institutions and militia for self-defense, laying the groundwork for later independence. By the 13th century, the St. Gotthard Pass became increasingly important for trade, enriching the central valley communities and increasing their political significance.

items.label: The Founding Cantons and Birth of the Swiss Confederation
items.summary: Three mountain communities sign an alliance that becomes the foundation of the Swiss state.
items.text: On August 1, 1291, Uri, Schwyz, and Unterwalden signed the Bundesbrief, a mutual defense pact against external threats and oppressive overlords—an act traditionally marking Switzerland's birth as an independent confederation. Over the next century and a half, additional cantons joined through military victories and diplomatic agreements, expanding the confederation. The Swiss proved formidable warriors, defeating the Habsburgs at battles like Sempach (1386), which established their reputation and encouraged more territories to seek membership. By 1450, the confederation had grown to eight cantons, each retaining significant autonomy while cooperating on matters of common defense and trade.

items.label: Expansion and the Golden Age
items.summary: The confederation grows wealthy and powerful through military victories and territorial expansion across the Alps.
items.text: Swiss mercenaries became famous across Europe for their martial prowess, commanding high wages and contributing wealth to their home cantons. The confederation expanded aggressively, conquering new territories including the Ticino south of the Alps, and incorporating additional cantons like Basel and Schaffhausen. Military victories against the Burgundians and Habsburgs enhanced Swiss prestige and security, making them one of Europe's most formidable military powers. However, the 1515 Battle of Marignano against the French marked the end of Swiss military expansion; Switzerland thereafter pursued a policy of armed neutrality and military non-engagement in foreign wars.

items.label: The Reformation and Religious Division
items.summary: Protestant reform divides the confederation into religious camps, sparking internal conflicts and civil wars.
items.text: When Martin Luther's ideas reached Switzerland, reformers like Huldrych Zwingli in Zurich transformed that canton into a Protestant stronghold, while other cantons remained Catholic. The religious split created deep divisions within the confederation, leading to the Kappel Wars (1529 and 1531) between Catholic and Protestant cantons—conflicts that killed thousands and almost destroyed the confederation. The Peace of Westphalia (1648) eventually recognized Switzerland's independence from the Holy Roman Empire and formally guaranteed its neutrality in European religious conflicts. This period of religious struggle ultimately forced the confederation to develop institutions and practices of tolerance and separation between secular and religious authority.

items.label: Era of Neutrality and Political Stability
items.summary: Switzerland becomes a recognized neutral power, prospering through trade and banking while avoiding major conflicts.
items.text: Declared neutral at Westphalia, Switzerland avoided the devastating wars that ravaged Europe, allowing it to focus on commerce, banking, and the development of sophisticated political institutions. Zurich, Geneva, and Bern became centers of intellectual life, hosting religious refugees and political thinkers who enriched the confederation's culture. The banking and textile industries flourished, with Swiss merchants and financiers gaining influence across Europe. Each canton developed its own traditions and institutions, creating a patchwork of republics, oligarchies, and democracies that nonetheless maintained sufficient unity to protect their collective independence.

items.label: French Revolution and the Helvetic Republic
items.summary: French revolutionary armies invade, abolish the old confederal system, and impose unified republican government.
items.text: The French Revolution shattered Switzerland's careful neutrality when Revolutionary armies invaded in 1798, abolishing the canton system and creating the centralized Helvetic Republic. This period of French occupation was traumatic for many Swiss, who resisted centralized authority and felt their traditional liberties threatened by revolutionary dogma. When Napoleon fell, the Congress of Vienna (1815) restored the cantonal system and guaranteed Swiss independence and perpetual neutrality—a settlement that shaped Swiss identity for the next two centuries. The traumatic experience of occupation unified the confederation around the principles of federalism, local autonomy, and armed neutrality.

items.label: Federal Constitution and Democratic Innovation
items.summary: Switzerland develops a federal system with progressive democratic reforms, becoming one of Europe's most stable and prosperous nations.
items.text: The 1848 Federal Constitution transformed Switzerland into a modern federal state with elected representative government, while the 1874 revision introduced direct democracy through referendums and initiatives. Industrial development during the 19th century made Switzerland wealthy through textiles, machinery, precision manufacturing, and banking, attracting investors and migrants from across Europe. The nation remained neutral during the Franco-Prussian War and other conflicts, focusing instead on internal development and social progress. By the early 20th century, Switzerland had become known for its political stability, high living standards, technological innovation, and banking secrecy—increasingly attracting international wealth and serving as a haven for political exiles.

items.label: Neutrality Through Two World Wars
items.summary: Switzerland navigates two devastating European conflicts while maintaining official neutrality and national independence.
items.text: Though surrounded by belligerents during World War I, Switzerland managed to remain neutral despite significant economic pressure and internal tensions between French-speaking and German-speaking populations. World War II tested Swiss neutrality even more severely, as the Nazi regime threatened invasion and Swiss banks handled looted gold and Jewish assets while the nation traded with both sides. The Swiss military's credible defense capability, along with economic utility to Nazi Germany, deterred invasion, though the moral compromises of neutrality would haunt the nation for decades. After 1945, Switzerland emerged unscathed while much of Europe lay in ruins, setting the stage for its postwar prosperity and international prominence.

items.label: Prosperity and Modern Integration
items.summary: Switzerland becomes a wealthy global financial hub while carefully managing its relationship with international institutions.
items.text: In the postwar era, Switzerland's banking sector expanded dramatically, making the nation one of the world's premier financial centers and a repository for global wealth. The country joined the United Nations in 2002 but deliberately remained outside the European Union to preserve its political autonomy and distinctive federal system. Despite economic integration with Europe and the world, Switzerland maintained its tradition of armed neutrality, high wages, generous social services, and meritocratic education. Today, Switzerland ranks among the world's highest in living standards, healthcare, education, and innovation, while grappling with modern challenges including immigration, climate change, and the pressure to reform banking secrecy laws to meet international standards.

guide/food.json.title: Food & Drink
guide/food.json.subtitle: How to eat well in Switzerland — customs, dishes and where to go.

items.label: Espresso is standard
items.text: When you order "Kaffee" in Switzerland, you'll get a small cup of strong espresso, not filter coffee. If you want a larger coffee with more milk, ask for a "Café Crème" or "Schale Kaffee" (a bowl of coffee).

items.label: Café culture is leisurely
items.text: Swiss cafés are for lingering. Order a small coffee and a pastry, sit for as long as you like, and no one will rush you. This is a social ritual, not a grab-and-go culture like some countries.

items.label: Independent neighbourhood cafés
items.text: Skip Starbucks entirely. Visit local cafés in residential areas where locals actually sit—you'll find better quality, authentic atmosphere, and no tourist markup. Every neighbourhood has a gem.

items.label: Afternoon coffee is serious
items.text: 4 o'clock coffee (Zvieri) with pastries is a real Swiss tradition, especially on weekends. Many cafés offer excellent cakes and tarts at this time.

items.label: Fondue
items.text: Melted cheese served in a communal pot where you dip bread cubes on long forks. It's as much about social interaction as food—order it for groups, not solo dining. Try Fondue Moitié-Moitié (half Gruyère, half Vacherin) for the classic blend.

items.label: Raclette
items.text: Melted cheese scraped onto a plate and eaten with potatoes, pickled onions, and cured meats. Unlike fondue, it's less communal and more individual, and equally satisfying on cold mountain evenings.

items.label: Rösti
items.text: Crispy fried potato pancakes, often topped with cheese, bacon, or a fried egg. This is the comfort food of Switzerland, especially in German-speaking regions, and appears on nearly every menu.

items.label: Älplermagronen
items.text: Alpine pasta with potatoes, cheese, onions, and sometimes bacon. It's hearty mountain food from the Appenzell region—filling and authentic, though heavy.

items.label: Geschnetzeltes (Zurich-style)
items.text: Thin strips of veal in a cream and white wine sauce, traditionally served with Rösti or rice. This is Zurich's signature dish and worth trying at a proper restaurant, not a tourist trap.

items.label: Basler Läckerli
items.text: A medieval spiced gingerbread from Basel made with honey, nuts, and candied fruit. Buy from local bakeries during Christmas season or year-round at specialty shops—authentic versions are complex and not overly sweet.

items.label: Avoid restaurant row in old towns
items.text: The main tourist areas (Marienplatz in Bern, Rathausplatz in Zurich) charge 40% more for mediocre food and service. Walk 10 minutes into residential neighbourhoods to find locals eating well at honest prices.

items.label: Beizli (neighborhood taverns)
items.text: These are unpretentious local pubs serving simple, excellent food. They're where Swiss people actually eat dinner—think wood panelling, friendly staff, and generous portions at fair prices.

items.label: Michelin-starred restaurants
items.text: Switzerland has exceptional fine dining (Basel, Zurich, Geneva especially). If you're splurging, book well ahead and expect innovative Swiss-inspired cuisine at world-class level. Budget 150–250 CHF per person.

items.label: Lunch is often better value
items.text: Many restaurants offer fixed-price lunch menus (Tagesmenu) at 15–25 CHF with proper dishes. Dinner prices jump significantly, so eat your main meal at lunch if budget matters.

items.label: Bakeries for breakfast
items.text: Swiss bakeries open early (6 or 7am) and sell fresh bread, pastries, and often simple sandwiches. Grab breakfast here instead of hotels—quality is better and cheaper.

items.label: Markets (Märkte)
items.text: Weekly farmers markets in every town sell fresh produce, bread, cheese, and prepared foods. In Basel, Zurich, and Bern, these are social hubs where locals shop and you can eat standing up for 5–8 CHF.

items.label: Regional menus vary by canton
items.text: German Switzerland favors hearty dishes like Rösti and sausages; French Switzerland (Romande) offers fondue and refined classics; Italian Switzerland (Ticino) has pasta and risotto. Know the region to understand the menu.

items.label: Plat du jour (daily special)
items.text: Most restaurants post a dish of the day (Tagesgericht or Tagesmenu). This is fresh, seasonal, and cheaper—always ask for it even if not listed prominently. It's often the best thing the kitchen makes.

items.label: Portion sizes are Swiss-sized
items.text: Portions are moderate by American standards but generous by European standards. Don't be confused by size; the food is rich and calorie-dense. You won't leave hungry.

items.label: Ask about regional cheeses
items.text: Servers can usually describe which local cheeses are available and how they're best eaten. Swiss cheese boards (Käseplatten) are exceptional—ask for a selection with bread and quince paste (Quitten-Paste).

items.label: Vegetarian options exist but are limited
items.text: Switzerland is meat-heavy. Vegetarian restaurants exist in major cities, but smaller towns offer limited options beyond salads and cheese dishes. Warn servers in advance if you have strict requirements.

items.label: Swiss wine is underrated and affordable
items.text: Switzerland produces excellent whites (Chasselas, Petite Arvine) and reds (Pinot Noir from Valais) that you rarely see exported. Order local wine in restaurants—it's often cheaper and better than imports and supports Swiss agriculture.

items.label: Chasselas (Dorin/Fendant)
items.text: The national white wine, light and crisp, perfect with cheese and raclette. Chasselas from Lavaux (UNESCO vineyards) or Valais is the real deal—try it to understand Swiss wine culture.

items.label: Mineral water is omnipresent
items.text: Swiss tap water is excellent, but restaurants push bottled water. Tap water (Leitungswasser) is free and better—request it and save money. If you want sparkling water, ask for "Mineralwasser mit Gas."

items.label: Wine bars and Weinstuben
items.text: These cosy wine taverns serve small glasses of local wine with simple food like cheese and charcuterie. They're where locals gather for evening aperitifs—very Swiss and unpretentious.

items.label: Appenzeller Bier or other regional beers
items.text: Each region has its own brewery. Appenzeller, Cardinal, Feldschlösschen are quality lagers. Local beer is fresher and cheaper than international brands—support regional breweries.

items.label: Spirits and digestifs are after-dinner
items.text: Grappa, Marc, and Kirsch (cherry brandy) are traditional digestifs, especially in French Switzerland. These are strong, expensive, and meant to be sipped slowly after meals, not shots.

items.label: Say hello and goodbye to staff
items.text: Always greet servers with "Grüezi" (German) or "Bonjour" (French) when entering and "Auf Wiedersehen" or "Au revoir" when leaving. This is basic respect and Swiss culture—ignoring it marks you as rude.

items.label: Don't snap fingers at servers
items.text: Making eye contact and a subtle gesture is acceptable; snapping is considered rude and American. Servers in Switzerland are professional and attentive—they'll notice you without being loud.

items.label: Tipping is optional but appreciated
items.text: Service and tax are included in menu prices. Rounding up or leaving 5–10% is customary for good service, but not mandatory. Leave cash tips on the table, not card tips—Swiss culture prefers cash.

items.label: Finish your plate or ask for a box
items.text: Leaving food is slightly wasteful in Swiss culture. If you can't finish, ask for a "Doggy bag" (Heimwegpackung) without shame—it's accepted and practical.

items.label: Dinner is typically 7–9pm
items.text: Swiss people eat dinner later than Americans but earlier than Southern Europeans. Arriving at 10pm will find many restaurants closing or unwelcoming. Book ahead for evening dining.

items.label: Fondue and raclette etiquette
items.text: When dipping bread in fondue, never double-dip. If you lose your bread to the pot, it's tradition to buy the next round of drinks. Keep the bread moving and the cheese warm by stirring gently.

guide/transport.json.title: Getting Around
guide/transport.json.subtitle: Trains, roads, taxis and how to move around Switzerland.

items.label: Reliability & frequency
items.text: Swiss Federal Railways (SBB/CFF) run trains every 15–30 minutes on major routes, even on Sundays. Trains are almost always on time; delays over 3 minutes are rare and qualify you for compensation via the SBB app or ticket counter.

items.label: Book in advance for discounts
items.text: Buying tickets 1–2 weeks ahead on SBB.ch can save 20–40% on long-distance trains. Last-minute bookings cost full price, but you won't lose money if you cancel within 15 minutes of purchase.

items.label: Validate tickets before boarding
items.text: All paper tickets must be stamped at orange machines on the platform before boarding, or you risk a 100 CHF fine. Digital tickets from the SBB app are automatically valid.
Rail strikes occasionally occur (typically 1–2 per year); check SBB.ch or the app for disruptions. During strikes, SBB often runs skeleton schedules and provides bus replacements.

items.label: Zone-based fares
items.text: Most Swiss cities use zone systems: Zurich (ZVV), Geneva (TPG), Basel (BVB) and Bern (Libero). One ticket covers all modes (bus, tram, metro, local trains) within those zones for 1 hour.

items.label: Real-time apps
items.text: Download the city operator's app (e.g., ZVV, Citynavi for Bern) or use Google Maps for live departure boards, delays and route planning. Apps usually show which exit to use for your destination.
Fare dodging carries a 100 CHF spot fine, plus potential legal follow-up. Inspectors conduct random checks, especially on trams and late-night buses.

items.label: Prams & luggage rules
items.text: Baby prams occupy a reserved area on most buses and trams; fold them or use the designated spaces. Large suitcases are allowed but should not block aisles.

items.label: Licensed taxi apps
items.text: Use Uber, Bolt, or Taxi 444 (Switzerland-specific). All are regulated and show the fare estimate before you book; cash payment is usually not an option.
Street hailing is rare; taxis are metered but difficult to flag down outside city centers. Always use an app or call your hotel concierge for a pre-booked taxi at 41 (0)44 forms number.

items.label: Tipping custom
items.text: Rounding up to the nearest 5 or 10 CHF is customary but not mandatory. You can tip through the app or leave cash.
Surge pricing on Uber/Bolt during peak hours (evenings, weekends) can double fares. Public transit is often faster and cheaper in cities.

items.label: Vignette requirement
items.text: All cars on Swiss motorways must display a annual vignette sticker (40 CHF). Rent cars pre-equipped or buy one at the border, petrol stations, or online; fines for non-compliance are 200 CHF.

items.label: Speed limits & enforcement
items.text: Motorways: 120 km/h, main roads: 100 km/h, towns: 50 km/h or 30 km/h zones. Speeding fines start at 40 CHF for minor violations and rise steeply; radar checks are frequent.
Drink-driving limit is 0.5 mg/ml (stricter than many EU countries). Breathalyzers are common at checkpoints; penalties include heavy fines and license suspension.

items.label: Alpine passes & parking
items.text: Many alpine passes (Gotthard, San Bernardino) are toll-free with a vignette. Winter tyres are mandatory November–March in Alpine regions; rental cars come equipped. Parking in cities is metered and expensive (5–15 CHF/hour).
Sunday driving bans for trucks mean reduced traffic but potential congestion around Saturday–Sunday transition. Plan mountain drives for early morning or avoid weekends.

items.label: Train connections
items.text: All three major Swiss airports have direct rail links: Zurich (SBB), Geneva (SNCF/SBB), and Basel (SBB). Trains depart every 15–30 minutes to city centers; journey times are 12–25 minutes and cost 15–20 CHF.

items.label: Arrival connections
items.text: Buy airport tickets at the train desk, machine, or SBB app before heading to the platform (located in the airport basement). No validation is needed for airport-purchased tickets.
Taxis from airports are expensive (60–100 CHF to city center). Ride apps (Uber, Bolt) offer only slightly cheaper alternatives. Train is the fastest and cheapest option.

items.label: Check-in & gate procedures
items.text: Arrive 2 hours before EU/Schengen flights and 3 hours for intercontinental flights. Airport signs are clear and multilingual; follow blue departure/baggage signs.

items.label: Scenic boat routes
items.text: Lake cruises on Zurich Lake, Lake Geneva, and Lake Lucerne are scenic alternatives to trains and included on many regional passes. Ferries are frequent (every 30–60 minutes) and run year-round on main routes.

items.label: Integrated ticketing
items.text: Most lake ferries accept the same regional pass (e.g., ZVV in Zurich, Libero in Bern). A standard zone ticket typically covers a short ferry journey within the same region.
Winter schedules (November–March) are reduced; check operator websites (ZSG, CGN, ZVV) for timetables. Some smaller routes close entirely in winter.

items.label: Swiss Travel Pass (multi-day)
items.text: Valid for 3, 4, 8 or 15 days; covers unlimited trains, buses, ferries, and metro/tram across Switzerland. Non-residents save 30–50% versus buying daily tickets. Buy online or at any train station.

items.label: City-specific passes
items.text: Zurich Card, Geneva Pass, and Bern Card include 72 hours of free city transit and museum discounts. Usually 72 CHF–100 CHF for 72 hours; worth it if you plan multiple museum visits and stay in the city.

items.label: Half-Fare Card
items.text: If staying longer than a week, buy a Half-Fare Card (165 CHF for a year) for 50% off all SBB/regional trains. Break-even is around 8–10 long-distance journeys.

items.label: How to buy tickets
items.text: Use the SBB app (fastest, digital receipt), self-service machines (accept card & cash), or ticket counters (for complex journeys). Airport and major stations have English-language machines.
Mobile ticket fraud is rare but possible; only buy from official SBB.ch, city operator apps, or physical machines. Third-party resellers (StubHub, etc.) are not recommended for Swiss transport.

items.label: Major providers & plans
items.text: Swisscom, Vodafone, and Salt are the three main carriers; all offer 5–10 GB tourist plans for 20–40 CHF per week. Prepaid SIM cards are sold at airport kiosks, train stations, and convenience stores.

items.label: WiFi availability
items.text: Free WiFi is common in hotels, cafes, and libraries (connect to SBB_Free at train stations). Most restaurants and shops require a Swiss mobile number or app login for WiFi access.
EU roaming works in Switzerland but charges 50–60 CHF per GB. A local SIM is much cheaper if you plan to stay more than a few days.

items.label: Offline maps & transit apps
items.text: Download offline maps (Google Maps, Maps.me) and the SBB app before traveling in areas with poor coverage (mountains, valleys). Transit apps work offline for reference but not real-time updates.
